title: 2025/12/02 - JU
displayTitle: 2025-12-02 - Justice Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Police
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Police
description: Tuesday 2 December 2025 | Justice Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Police Submitters: New Zealand Police Richard Chambers, Commissioner of Police Jill Rogers, Deputy Commissioner: Northern Tusha Penny, Deputy Commissioner (acting) Andrea Conlan, Chief Operating Officer Mike Pannett, Assistant Commissioner Mike Johnson, Assistant Commissioner Corrie Parnell, Assistant Commissioner Uraia Vakaruru, Assistant Commissioner Jeremy Wood, Chief Policy and Planning Officer Leigh MacDonald, Chief People Officer Matt Winter, Chief Information Officer Paul LaPlanche, Chief Financial Officer Ben Butterfield, Director Frontline Enablement Richard Wilson, Director Firearms Transformation Steve Ferguson, NGCC Lead Entity Director Hon Mark Mitchell, Minister of Police
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Police. New Zealand Police. Richard Chambers, Commissioner of Police

chunks.text: Okay, uh, commissioner, um, welcome everyone, um, welcome to the New Zealand Police. Uh, we've got three hours with you, commissioner, and your team.

It's a long time. Um, we have a structured agenda, so I'm going to try and keep us within that structure. You'll have it. Um, and I would ask, as we get questions and different people t- have to answer them, please just feel free to come up and speak into the microphone because it makes it so much easier for- for us to hear, uh, some of the answers. So, on that note, Commissioner, apparently you've got six minutes.

(laughs) Right. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Uh, look, uh, can I firstly introduce, introduce my colleagues beside me? Andrea Connell, chief operating officer, acting Deputy Commissioner Jill Rogers, and acting deputy, Deputy Commissioner Tasha Penny. Thanks for the opportunity today. Um, can I just acknowledge, firstly, all of the committee members here with us and the rest of my team and, and other people here in the room?

Um, the last time I was here at the annual review was five days into my time as the commissioner of police. Well, it's been a big year, frankly. Um, it started in the worst possible way with the loss of, uh, Lyn Fleming on the 1st of January, and it's been pretty tough, frankly, uh, ever since. But, um, I'm very, very proud of the team that I have supporting me, uh, my colleagues across the country, uh, and I thank, uh, not only them for the work they do at the front line, but also, um, the public of New Zealand and everybody in this room because everybody does

appreciate that policing isn't easy. And we've had our challenges, not only obviously starting so, so sadly with the loss of Lyn, but many other things that we've had to navigate through the year. Uh, and most recently, of course, the IPCA report. That gives us plenty to think about, uh, moving forward. So, um, uh, there's a lot to be very, very proud of though, and as the commissioner of police, I am very, very proud of the results that we have seen over the last 12 months in terms of policing gangs.

I think about the 500 and how hard we have worked, um, to achieve that target, uh, as early as we can, but making sure we recruit the right people into New Zealand Police for our country. I think about the differences that we have made, uh, in retail crime, uh, and, and many, many other things. Uh, government targets, youth violence, um, uh, the infl- the impact that we've had with our increase in beat patrols. Um, certainly the expectations that I outlined on day one as the commissioner, um, have proven to be the right things and they will

remain firmly our priorities moving forward. Um, we certainly have a lot to, to think about in 2026. Um, I'm looking very, very forward to, uh, having a new leadership team, uh, supporting me help steer New Zealand Police and the colleagues, uh, across the country who do an outstanding job day and night. Um, amongst, uh, a number of the things that we need to think about, and I know it's first on the agenda, is of course the audit report, and I welcome the, uh, areas of focus there, whether that's contract management,

asset management, procurement, um, or the breach of appropriation that we had in road policing. Those four things in particular, um, we, uh, are already working hard to be better at. Um, uh, of course, uh, given the events of this year, trust and confidence is, is an absolute priority for me. I said that on day one, nothing has changed. That matters.

We've taken some hits and we will make sure we move forward and, uh, do our absolute best for our country. Uh, amongst other things for 2026, um, I've been clear, uh, with my executives that, um, those four priorities I articulated here a year ago remain. That is focus on core policing, supporting the front line, uh, leadership, and our role, uh, being connected to the front line, um, our visibility, and of course, our accountability under leadership are as important, if not more so than they were 12 months ago. And of course, our fiscal

responsibility, ensuring that New Zealand Police... uh, the New Zealand public can have confidence in New Zealand Police in terms of vote police and our little over $3 billion that we have to deliver services to the country. Um, I, uh, through the year, I have also outlined three particular goals, which are fairly simple. Uh, s- service, safety, and trust.

Those three things matter to all New Zealanders and they matter to us, uh, in policing as a result of expectations. I have also, um, started early work on putting in place some of our own internal targets that, uh, I want out my organization, uh, to aspire to, which will, uh, drive, uh, behaviors.

Uh, and those targets are 80%, uh, trust and confidence. Uh, we've been there before. There's no reason we can't do it again. Um, uh, we have seen some small incremental increases. We've taken some hits on trust and confidence, um, particularly recently, but I want to set an expectation that we, um, achieve 80%.

And that will take time, but we'll work really hard towards it. Another, um, another target that I want to put in place for the organization is 80%, um, satisfaction for the services that we deliver. We're currently sitting at about 71%, so we've got some work to do there too, but I know that we can do it. Uh, and also, uh, 15%, uh, increase in resolutions for retail crime in particular. We have seen incredible work across the country t- addressing retail crime. Uh, and I want that to continue.

Um, and then a 15% reduction in public place violence. So, uh, that's, uh, particularly important in CBDs across New Zealand. Uh, and that in particular drives behavior for beat patrols and visibility of police. Many initiatives, uh, um, underway across the organization to help us be, uh, our absolute best for the country. Uh, one of the things that I have, um, particularly focused on more recently is, uh, a thing called, called Frontline Service Model.

And that is that we have over 600 sworn staff who aren't in necessarily frontline roles, uh, and creating expectations for them to also get out and make a contribution to policing on the front line. Increases our presence across the country. Um-Lots of things to be thinking about. Uh, I know that we can get there. It's been a tough year, but I, I, I think and, and I welcome questions from the committee, um, uh, uh, one of the, one of the final points that I'm, I'm very, very proud of is that if I look at the, uh, diversity that we have brought into New Zealand Police over the last five

years, in particular, um, Maori communities, our Pacific communities and ethni- ethnic communities, if I think about Maori communities in particular over the last five years, we've had a 20% increase in Maori colleagues in New Zealand Police and I think that's a success story. And I said in this very place a year ago that that matters, and it's the responsibility of all members of, uh, New Zealand Police, uh, in particular the leadership, it's a value that we all subscribe to that we find better ways to achieve outcomes for all communities across New Zealand.

So thanks, uh, thanks for everyone's time today and I welcome questions. Thank you, uh, Commissioner, and I acknowledge the work of the police. I should say that at the outset. Right. First question from, uh, Honorable Trinny Anderson. Thank you very much, uh, thank you Commissioner. What work is being carried out by police to mitigate the cost pressures and deliver the 500 new police?

Uh, well, we, um, certainly started a year ago, um, uh, with a lot of our cost-saving initiatives. In fact, even, uh, one of the first things I did was a na- a restructure of the executive to trim down and slim down and actually re-identify opportunities to reinvest. Um, that, uh, I've been very clear that with the initiatives that we've rolled out over the last 12 months, that that doesn't impact on the front line.

Front line is New Zealand Police and my job, along with my executive is to, is to support them. So we've, uh, we've had a very close look at the opportunities to not only save but also to reinvest and I'm particularly proud of where we, uh, have made savings, in particular contractors and consultants, uh, with a saving of r- um, close to $90 million over the last, um, uh, a little over a year. Um, those are all opportunities to reinvest. So we're, we're looking really, really hard, um, we're... It's one of our priorities, fiscal responsibility.

And, and when will the 500 be delivered? Um, as soon as possible. I did say, of course, a year ago that, um, uh, we were working really hard to reach the 500 but that I wasn't prepared to compromise, um, on the, uh, caliber of New Zealand Police officer. Um, uh, this year if... to give you a couple of statistics, we... over the last 12 months, we've had over...

actually close to 9,000 applications. Uh, the 12 months prior to that was a little over 5,000. Um, this year we will have, a- as Christmas approaches, nearly, uh, have, um, signed on another 900 staff. That's about 800 brand new recruits and about close to 100 rejoins.

Mm-hmm. Uh, we've never achieved that many in a 12-month period and we've worked incredibly hard to, um, to promote New Z- uh, policing, uh, in New Zealand as a career and e- even with some of our colleagues who have gone across to Australia, we've had some big successes with them coming home. So working really, really hard, um, I'm hopeful that we'll get there, uh, as, as early as possible in 2026 but I...

as I've, as I've always said, I, I, I'm not about to compromise on the standards, uh, of the, uh, So, um, so from the calculations, the p- police to population ratio ha- has, has dropped over the course of this government. There was funding in place for one police officer to every 480 New Zealanders. That's now changed to around one police officer to every 510 New Zealanders.

Do you, do you have any particular explanation for that increase given your focus on increased police visibility? Yeah, look, I think this, this, this week, uh, 10,451 sworn staff, uh, in a couple of weeks' time we'll have another, uh, 80 graduate and few, um, a month after that and, and 91 we've got a target of 500. What I'm focused on is making sure that we achieve the 500 target but alongside that as I spoke about is an in- is an initiative to ensure that sworn staff, whatever their role in New Zealand Police, particularly those that aren't, uh, front line as

a core function, but they also get out and make a contribution. So I'm drawing on all the levers that I can internally to ensure that we, uh, we do our best and, and visibility does matter and over 600 staff that are sworn but non-front line, uh, all have a contribution to make just like I do and I'll be out there as a policeman doing my bit Do you intend to restore that one to 480 ratio that was previously funded?

Uh, that, well, that's, that's not so much a decision for me. W- what is for me is to ensure that I, um, uh, we, we work really, really hard to recruit that 500 and ma- and optimize the, the total actual strength of New Zealand Police officers. That's what I've got to focus on because-... w- we can have- Just one last thing.... we can have statistics about, you know, ratios and things like that but I, I've got to take what's in front of me and that is 500 new staff and making sure that my existing staff are all making a contribution.

The, um, the annual report indicates there's been a decline in general public who agree that police are effectively responding to crime. Uh, do you think that has a correlation to the drop in the police to population ratio? Sorry, say that again, sorry? So in your annual report, there's been a decline when people are asked, the general public, "Do you agree that police are effectively responding to serious crime?"

That's dropped in your annual report over the past couple of years. Right. Do you think there's a link between that decrease and the fact that there are fewer police per other peop- per population in New Zealand? Yeah, well it's i- it's interesting, a lot of the feedback that I've had throughout the year given our emphasis on beat patrols and visibility is that in fact pretty much across all communities the feedback I'm getting is that we see a lot more police out there, uh, whether that's on the roads or on the beat.

So th- uh, that's my experience, uh, and certainly that's the experience that I'm drawing from members of the public that tell me, putting aside ratios. In terms of our responsiveness, uh, I mean, that is directly related to the priorities that I've set about being a responsive police service, um, service being one of our goals. So we will w- we will work incredibly hard to provide the best possible service, uh, ac- across all the demands that police has to manage.

Okay. We're gonna take two more sups and then we'll move on to a new question. Uh, Mr. Bates. Thanks, Chair. Just going back, Commissioner, to your comment regarding the number of rejoins from Australia. Mm-hmm. What is driving those officers coming home? And what more are you doing to bring more of those- Yeah. ... officers back to New Zealand? Yeah. Thank you, good question. I, um, have spoken to many of those who either have returned to New Zealand or intend to return to New Zealand, and, uh, in a, in a way, it actually happened by accident when I was

over there and I spoke to some that had, had decided to go to Australia. Um, the common theme from pretty much all of them, and as most recently as Sunday night when I spoke to one who is currently with Queensland Police but wants to return, um, in the next few months- Right.... is that, yes, while financially it may be a different story to tell, um, when, when you go and experience policing in a different place, you see, uh, what it can be like, um, when you think about the tools that some do or don't have, as the case may be in Australia, to help them police, uh, the

camaraderie, um, the emphasis that's placed on working with all communities, in particular for our country, the pride that we have in working with Maori. Um, there's a whole host of reasons, and those are just a selection of some of the, uh, responses that I do get.

But it's fairly consistent when I ask the why, as to why they wanna come home. And having, uh, been offshore myself, and I just returned on Sunday night from, uh, Morocco where I attended the Interpol's General Assembly, uh, the biggest event for police chiefs around the world, um, oh, I'm constantly reminded of how lucky we are to live in New Zealand, to be Kiwis, and for me as a policeman, uh, alongside 15,000 colleagues who do a, an incredibly, um, good job. Actually, when you see it outside your own country, you realize how lucky we are.

So those are the common themes that I hear wherever I go and whoever I speak to. Great. And, uh, just a second sup regarding the measures that the member was just referring to that were tracking down from 2020, uh, 78, 77% for those two references for people's feeling of crime- (clears throat)... coming down to 71, 73 when, when the government became the government, stabilizing.

What's your forecast for those, um, indicators over the coming year? In terms of our response? In terms of the percentage of people who agree or strongly agree that police that deal effectively with serious crime- Yeah.... and the second one's most harmful, both of those tracking down from the high 70s- Yeah.... uh, 2020, to 71, 73- Yeah.

... when we became the government. Yes. Yeah, well- That in mind, what's your forecast? So, forec- hard to predict, uh, what the future might looks like- Yeah.... other than to be able to talk to the priorities that I've set, the importance of trust and confidence, our visibility in communities across the country, and how hard we are working to be as present as possible and, and respond to all the different demands on New Zealand Police.

So, uh, our intent is the right intent, and we hope that those, um, statistics, um, get better. Right. I'm going to Miss Paul. Uh, just remind members, audit, we're talking about the audit here. Yep. Sorry, Mr. Chair. We're working through the structure- Yep.... moving this forward. Yeah. Um, kia ora, uh, Commissioner. My question is around, um, uh, your executive restructure and how that's going.

So I understand that that's a process that's happening at the moment, or- Uh-... where, where are we at with that? Yes, look, I did a restructure, uh, very, uh, early on in my tenure where we trimmed it down. I felt that it was too big, too bloated, too expensive, and not delivering for the organization or New Zealand, so I trimmed down. Um, and at the moment, uh, we are going through processes to appoint, uh, new deputy commissioners that will inevitably result in appointments at assistant commissioner level and also district commander level.

So really in the top, um, three layers of New Zealand Police, we are going through processes to put new leadership in place, and I'm really looking forward to that- Mm-hmm.... and starting 2026 with a new team and substantive people in, in positions. Uh, I've managed through the year without that.

So basically about simplifying the executive structure, is that- Making it, certainly making it more lean, but not changing any of our focus, uh, the things that we value. None of that is changing. It's simply saying that where there are savings potentially to be made, what do those look like, how can we achieve them, how can we reinvest them?

(clears throat) 100% (whispers) . So, um, priorities don't change with, with a change in structure. Um, uh, roles and responsibilities change because then inevitably what it means is senior leaders pick up more because there's less to pick up often the same workload, but we're up for that. Um, our, our value set doesn't change.

Um, um, but, uh, having people in positions and confirmed matters, and I'm, I'm looking forward to that. Yeah, that's really helpful because obviously the executive structure is gonna dictate how your operations work. Um, I have, um, an OIA around the executive restructure that, um, says that you're considering disestablishing the deputy chief executive iwi and communities role within the restructure. Is that something you're considering?

No, that, that happened a year ago. Yeah. And why did you do that? Uh, that was about trimming down the executive level. As I've s- as I've said, it doesn't change the value set that we have. In fact, um, if I think about that particular role, it places greater emphasis on, uh, who is in the executive to, to deliver on expectations. And, and, and one of our, um, core values is commitment to Maori and the treaty.

Um, we wear it on our shoulders every day. Um, I'm very passionate about that. In fact, it's one of the things that, in my travels and work offshore, I think New Zealand can be proud of. (clears throat) Doesn't, doesn't change the emphasis that we place on that and I... And as I said in this room a year ago-... um, you know, the work that we do with all communities across New Zealand, particularly at a grassroots level, our, uh, iwi liaison offices, our Maori responsiveness managers, uh, across all our structures, both operationally and corporate, we all

subscribe to the same values. So, in the restructure that I did earlier this year, uh, that position did g- get taken out along with, um, a number of others at tier three as well. Um, doesn't change the emphasis and expectation we put on each other to, um, to step up. So, that's picked back up through the iwi liaison offices, or is there a senior-... person in your structure that is in charge of liaising with communities and with iwi?

Well, we all are. I mean, whilst we have different roles and responsibilities, a huge amount of my time as commissioner is spent engaging with communities. I'm very lucky to have focus forums that provide good advice and support to me. Okay, um- Uh, sorry. I'm going to go to Mr. Ferris now. We're just talking about audit results, Mr. Ferris. Um, your question, please.

Yeah, kia ora. Kia ora, Commissioner. Um, hey, you've noted yourself and the OAG report noted that trust and confidence is pretty much at an all-time low in the police. It's down to 69%, hasn't been there since 2020. Um, now, do you have, or what, what's the equivalent trust level amongst the Maori, Maori community specifically?

Yeah, look, traditionally, um, trust and confidence across Maori communities has been lower than that, and it's an area for us to, to work on. Uh, 60%, in the 60s is the, is the figure lower than the ones that you just, um, mentioned. Uh, that gives us, uh, uh, lots to think about. Um, I'm very conscious of that. But, you know, I do also know and acknowledge that many of the initiatives that we've put in place across the organization, Te Paerenga being one of those, are delivering, uh, results for Maori communities that we

can all actually be very, very proud of. And, um, when, when I think about how we, how police agencies around the world work with communities, I think New Zealand leads the way globally. Yeah, kia ora. Hey, look, six months ago at the last scrutiny, uh, discussion you and I had, and the minister, you both dismissed the relevance of the understanding police delivery report for Maori.

Um, given, you know, where we are currently and the recent revelations around the conduct and attitude of the most senior police officers in the country, um, and the connection to what Maori experience at the hands of the police, do you think rejecting that report was a mistake? Uh, it's not, it's not that we rejected the report. What, what I did say was that- Oh, I'm pretty, I'm pretty clear that you did reject the port, the report, both of you- Yeah.

... because it was the last executive's report, not your own. No, look, what I did say that there are certainly some learnings in there for us, and that's why the executive has gone through all 51 recommendations. We've adopted 15 of them, we've put 29 of them, uh, on hold while we, um, prioritize, uh, and we have rejected seven of them.

So, we've gone through- Okay, but just given, given that low, um, given the low trust and confidence in the Maori communities, what are your targets to improve that through the, uh, through 2026? So, of the 15 we've adopted, and this will also apply to many of the 29, we, we are blending that into Te Hiranga o te Tai, which you'll be familiar with, and the three pou that matter there.

One of those is cultural competency, one of them is around partnerships, and the other is about initiatives and making sure that we work together with Maori to deliver better outcomes. And I, and I know that as an organization, we can be very proud of how hard we've worked in this space. And as I say, it's not just about bosses, uh, particularly here in Wellington, it's about all staff who subscribe to the same value set and, and, and the staff that we have at an operational level, um, in Maori Pacific and Ethnic Services.

Collectively, we actually are, are working very, very hard and that isn't gonna change one bit. Okay, thank you for- Hey, just last, just lastly, Mr. Ferris. Mr. Ferris.... the OAG, the OAG report- Mr. Ferris. Yeah, this last, last sup on this one. Um, we've... This is the last sup, but I'm gonna remind you that we have a section on Maori, uh, partnerships later at, on, and I just wanna make sure we could k- keep this on the agenda. So last sup, please.

Yeah, yep, yep. Yep, yep, thanks for that. Um, okay, so the OAG also found that there was weak outcome reporting for Maori initiatives. Uh, h- how are you gonna bolster the activity by having clear targets for police? (clears throat) Uh, clear targets for police?

Or specifically to Maori? Yeah, the, the OAG report found weak outcome reporting around Maori achievement or advancement. Right, So, weak outcome reporting for Maori. So, how are you gonna fix that? I, I think we need to celebrate successes better and that's certainly something that in the new year we need to look at, because the results are there. Uh, if I think about the high percentage of non-reoffending rate in Te, uh, Te Paerenga as one example, the fact that we've got 12 Rangatahi, uh, TPOs, uh, opportunities across the

country. We've got 30 for, for adults. It's all there, perhaps we just don't celebrate it enough. Because if I, uh, think about the progress that we've made in, as an organization over 30 years in particular, we can't dismiss how much hard work's been put into this, and that won't be changing under my leadership. Right. Now, we're gonna move on to organizational health, and the first one's strategic direction. I'm gonna come back to you, so hopefully you can weave your question into this. We're gonna start with Honorable Ginny Anderson.

Thank you, Andrew. Uh, Commissioner, do you think that there is a problem with police culture? No, I don't. Um, what we have seen in the last 12 months is some, some events, uh, particularly recently with the IPCA report, which relates to, uh, conduct of, uh, largely former members of the most, at the most senior level.

And we've had some other, um, experiences that we've identified, um, through either own, our own audits and checking processes, whether that's breath testing devices or it's access to inappropriate material. Though, those two, two, two last, um, situations in particular are as consequence of the decisions that me and my executive have made to, to have a closer look.

Um, that, uh, is very disappointing. Um, that doesn't mean that the entire organization should be branded in a way that's unfair, because it's not.... uh, what anyone, uh, deserves. Th- those that do, uh, fall below expectations, um, my expectation is that we act on that and we, we deal with them. Um, and I'm confident that albeit hugely disappointing, um, uh, it's a reminder to all staff that, uh, expectations are there and we'll act on anything that falls below standards.

The IPCA have been very clear that they do believe there is a problem with police culture. Are you taking the position that the IPCA is incorrect in that assessment? No. I've taken on board already all the recommendations. But I think what, what I'm saying is that would be h- would be grossly unfair to, to reflect on 15,000 men and women who do a tough job and a very good job across the country day and night. That, that's what I'm saying.

It's, it's, it ... but there, if there are things, uh, that have been identified, and the IPCA report certainly did within that, the cohort of people that were subject to part, to that investigation certainly identified an issue. Um, and I've taken all of those recommendations on board. My team, we've put together a plan that, um, we're prioritizing in terms of effort. We've got many things underway and we've got additional things underway, such as the performance improvement review that I, I'm working with Public Service Commission on to identify other areas of opportunity, whether that's

integrity or culture or conduct. So, we, we are certainly working very, very hard to, um, to correct behaviors that fall short of expectations, not just for me as the Commissioner of Police, but for all New Zealanders, frankly. So just to be clear, the, the IPCA specifically said that after the Basley report there were some, um, improvements made but there was still much more to be done- Yeah.

... for police culture in general. Yeah. Are you saying that you don't think- No.... that that applies? Yeah. I wouldn't be accepting all the recommendations if I didn't agree with them, so there are certainly pockets of opportunity and that's why, that's why we're doing what we're doing, whether it's, uh, the auditing processes to identify those pockets and so that gives us the opportunity to act on. But the vast majority of police, um, uh, turn up do a, a, a fantastic job and it's, uh, it's expected that we would deal with anything

that falls short and that's, that's exactly what we're doing. Did you consider having one of your new deputies as non-constabulary as there was post the Basley inquiry? Uh, I think the precedent was Lyn Provo. Yeah. That's ... We are, we're working through some options. That's the sorts of things. I mean that, that, that's, um ... You know, there's all sorts of things on the table but, you know, I'm looking forward to having a new leadership team in place.

There are very, very capable, um, experienced and high caliber, uh, colleagues that are ready to step up. We've just gotta let processes take their course. What changes have you made, uh, to police or how have you changed police culture, uh, during the 2024/'25 financial year?

Uh, well, set expectations from the outset and I've, I've done quite a bit of that this year, um, whether that's, uh, involving the police college or it's involving things like retail crime or it's, um, outlining those four priorities that I've spoken about, if it's the goals, service, safety, trust. Like there's many different artifacts or conversations that I can point to, many of which I have led, uh, that have reinforced what I expect, uh, of my organization and the people in it.

Sorry. Last one, sorry. And the process to appoint the new deputy commissioners, is that different to ones that have been run in the past or the same process? No. Look, I th- well, I mean, I ... The current process that's underway is not led by me. That's led by the Public Service Commission. Um, but the work that's going in to ensure that all the right probity, integrity and all those other checks that New Zealanders would expect when you're appointing, um, people at the most senior level of the organization, that is very, uh, intense, uh, and it needs to be.

So that's the difference. And do you think- Uh, no, wait. No, no, no. We've got one supplementary from Mr. Rutherford. Thank you, Mr. Chair. In, in light of the line of questioning from the member, is it fair to say that part of your role and responsibility is to flush out what effectively were the bad eggs- Yes.... and it's unfair to tar everybody in the police-... organization with the same brush?

Yes. Correct. Um, you know, I've spoken to a lot of my staff throughout the entire year and, um, uh, whether you describe it as anger or just being let down, the behaviors of a small number, um, it's unfair to, for that to reflect on 15,000 people who do an outstanding job for our country. Yeah. Thank you. Yeah. Okay. I'm going to Mr. Waititi.

Thank you very much. And, and just on the, um, the, the back end of, uh, (foreign language) to you, uh, Commissioner, and welcome to you, um, around police culture, and I just want to touch on the understanding of policing delivery report. Mm-hmm. Um, in the 2024/2025 annual report provides extensive performance tables but does not clearly set out how police are measuring and reducing disproportionate impacts on Maori at key decision s- points, um, stops to arrests- Yeah.

... use of force, charging decisions. Yeah. What recommendations from understanding policing delivery have been accepted, partially accepted or rejected, and why? Yeah. Okay. Look. I'll, I'm gonna call on, um, Acting Assistant Commissioner Urai Wakaururu who has particular focus on a daily basis on these, this level of detail. But while, uh, UV comes here, look, as I, as I have said, um, Sir, the, um, executive has considered all 51 recommendations.

We have adopted 15 of them, uh, released an implementation. There is 29 there were on hold because we do need to prioritize, um, and seven that we, that we, uh, have rejected.
